title: Scalability Fundamentals
summary: Horizontal vs vertical scaling, stateless design, replication, partitioning, and the key principles behind scalable systems.

sections.content:
## Scalability Defined

Scalability is the ability of a system to **handle increased load** without degrading performance. Load can mean: requests per second, concurrent users, data volume, or message throughput.

### Two approaches to scaling:
- **Vertical scaling (Scale Up)** — add more power to existing machines (more CPU, RAM, faster disk)
- **Horizontal scaling (Scale Out)** — add more machines to distribute load

sections.title: Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling
sections.content:
graph LR
    subgraph Vertical Scale Up
        S1[Server\n4 CPU 16GB] -->|upgrade| S2[Server\n32 CPU 256GB]
        Note1[Single point of failure!\nHard ceiling]
    end
    subgraph Horizontal Scale Out
        LB[Load Balancer]
        LB --> N1[Node 1]
        LB --> N2[Node 2]
        LB --> N3[Node 3]
        LB --> NN[Node N...]
    end
    style S2 fill:#d97706,color:#fff
    style LB fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## Stateless Design

The key to horizontal scalability is making your application **stateless** — no request-local state stored in memory on the server.

**Problem with stateful servers:**
```
User logs in → Session stored in Server A memory
Next request → Load balancer routes to Server B
Server B has no session → User is logged out!
```

**Stateless solution:**
```
User logs in → JWT token issued, stored in browser
Next request → JWT attached → ANY server can validate it
All servers are identical → scale freely
```

### Externalizing state:
- **Session data** → Redis, cookie/JWT
- **File uploads** → S3/object storage
- **Cache** → Redis cluster, Memcached
- **DB connections** → Connection pooling (PgBouncer)

sections.title: Stateless Architecture
sections.content:
graph TB
    Client --> LB[Load Balancer]
    LB --> A1[App Server 1]
    LB --> A2[App Server 2]
    LB --> A3[App Server 3]
    A1 --> Redis[(Redis\nSessions/Cache)]
    A2 --> Redis
    A3 --> Redis
    A1 --> DB[(PostgreSQL\nPrimary)]
    A2 --> DB
    A3 --> DB
    style LB fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style Redis fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style DB fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Key Scalability Patterns

### Read Replicas
Scale read-heavy workloads by replicating data to read replicas:
```
Writes → Primary DB
Reads  → Read Replica 1, 2, 3 (round-robin or closest)
```

### Caching
Cache frequently-read, rarely-changed data at multiple layers:
- **CDN cache** — static assets, public API responses at edge
- **Application cache** — Redis for computed results, sessions
- **DB query cache** — memoize expensive queries

### Async Processing
Offload slow operations to queues:
```
HTTP Request → validate → enqueue → return 202 Accepted
Worker → dequeue → process slowly → update DB
```

sections.title: Start simple, scale when needed
sections.content: Don't over-engineer scalability from day one. A single well-tuned PostgreSQL instance can handle thousands of requests/second. Add replicas, caches, and queues when you have measured evidence you need them.

sections.question: Why is stateless application design critical for horizontal scaling?
options: Stateless apps use less memory per request
Any server can handle any request without needing session data from a specific server
Stateless apps don't need load balancers
Stateless apps are faster because they don't store anything
sections.explanation: With stateless design, no server holds user-specific in-memory state. Any request can be routed to any server by the load balancer. This enables adding/removing servers freely, auto-scaling, and surviving individual server failures without losing user sessions.
